title: How to Set Up a Printer on Linux
description: Connect and configure your printer on Linux — plug-and-play setup, manual configuration, and troubleshooting common issues.

steps.title: Try plug and play first
steps.content: Many modern printers work out of the box on Linux. Connect your printer via USB or make sure it's on the same WiFi network, then open **Settings → Printers**. Click **Add Printer** and your system should detect it automatically. HP, Brother, and Epson printers have the best Linux support.

steps.title: Install printer drivers
steps.content: If your printer isn't detected automatically, install the common driver packages. These cover most consumer printers.
steps.code:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install cups printer-driver-all

# For HP printers specifically:
sudo apt install hplip

# For Brother printers:
# Download the driver from support.brother.com and run the installer

steps.title: Start the CUPS print service
steps.content: CUPS (Common Unix Printing System) manages all printing on Linux. Make sure it's running.
steps.code:
sudo systemctl enable cups
sudo systemctl start cups
sudo systemctl status cups

steps.title: Add a printer via the web interface
steps.content: CUPS has a powerful web interface for printer management. Open it in your browser and follow the wizard to add your printer.
steps.code:
# Open in your browser:
xdg-open http://localhost:631

steps.title: Add a network printer manually
steps.content: If your WiFi printer isn't detected, you can add it by IP address. Find your printer's IP from its settings menu or your router's device list.
steps.code:
# Find printers on your network:
avahi-browse -art | grep Printer

# Or add directly via CUPS:
lpadmin -p MyPrinter -E -v ipp://192.168.1.100/ipp/print -m everywhere

steps.title: Print a test page
steps.content: Verify everything works by printing a test page from the terminal or from Settings → Printers → your printer → Print Test Page.
steps.code:
# Print a test page:
lp -d MyPrinter /usr/share/cups/data/testprint

# Or print any file:
lp -d MyPrinter document.pdf

seo.title: How to Set Up a Printer on Linux — USB & WiFi Guide
seo.description: Step-by-step guide to setting up printers on Linux. Covers plug-and-play, CUPS configuration, HP/Brother/Epson drivers, and network printers.
